title: The Build-vs-Buy Boundary

content.statement: Build it in-house only until buying it on the market costs you less.
content.explainer: A company exists because doing some things internally beats paying the market's costs of finding, negotiating, and policing a supplier, but that advantage has an edge. Grow past the point where internal cost exceeds the market's, and you're keeping work you should be buying.
questions: Which functions are you doing in-house that a specialist could deliver cheaper once you count your own coordination time?
What are the hidden transaction costs, contracting, monitoring, quality risk, of the things you currently outsource?
As you grow, which internal task has quietly become more expensive to run than to simply buy?
sop: Pick one function you currently perform in-house, support, fulfillment, design, or bookkeeping.
Add up its true internal cost: labor, tools, overhead, and your own coordination time.
Get real market quotes and add the transaction costs of buying, vetting, contracting, monitoring, quality risk.
Compare internal cost to market transaction cost and keep it in-house only while internal is cheaper (see calculation, page 6).
Re-run this test as you scale, since the cheaper side flips as volume and complexity change.

definitions.def: The full cost of making an exchange on the market: finding a supplier, negotiating, writing and enforcing contracts, and monitoring the quality you get.
The idea that companies exist because handling some tasks internally is cheaper than buying them on the open market, and should grow only until that stops being true.

content.equation: Firm Boundary: Internal Cost = Market Transaction Cost
content.equation_example: In-house: production $4,000 + management $2,000 = $6,000/mo. Buying: price $4,500 + contracting $1,000 = $5,500/mo. $6,000 > $5,500, so the market is cheaper, outsource it. When your internal cost falls below the market's $5,500, bring it back in-house.
content.closing_statement: The right size for anything is the point where doing it yourself stops being worth it.
content.question: Which functions are you doing in-house that a specialist could deliver cheaper once you count your own coordination time?

caption:
Principle Nº 337: The Build-vs-Buy Boundary

Build it in-house only until buying it on the market costs you less.

Ask yourself: Which functions are you doing in-house that a specialist could deliver cheaper once you count your own coordination time?
